content:
Good afternoon, folks. Happy Saturday to you. We're going to do a little bit of a review today.
Here's the Dollar Index on our weekly chart. There is a Fair Value Gap (FVG) that has been left in place
from last week's trading, which is this weekly candle. I want to see if we can keep this level in the form of
resistance. In other words, price can go up here, hit it, and how we trade after we go into it is going to be
vital for me going forward.
- If we were to go above the FVG, come back down, I would expect it to act as support.
- Maybe reach up into the rejection block, which is this highest up-close price here.
- Eventually target the high and the consequent encroachment of that wick.

section_title: UPCOMING TRADING SESSION EXPECTATIONS FOR DXY
content:
I want to wait and see what we get on Sunday's opening, and I'm going to allow Monday's trading, because
there's a lot of things happening around the world. If you're not paying attention, these things might
surprise you. Just check the news headlines and you'll see what I'm talking about.
Moving on into a daily chart, so there is our weekly Fair Value Gap viewed with a daily Candlestick chart. It
doesn't look like there's any imbalance there at all, but on the weekly chart, there is.
We did have some support off of this Fair Value Gap here. I mentioned when we were dropping go back
and look at the other commentaries that we would reach into here at a Volume Imbalance (VI) instead of
a couple of that. Then this is the area to watch. We traded down into that on Thursday, rallied up into the
consequent encroachment of this wick right there. So it's a wait and see for Sunday's trading and Monday's
trading. I keep my cards close to my vest; I'm not certain what we'll see, but it could be pretty volatile next
week.
EUR/

Here's the Euro Dollar daily chart. We have a weekly imbalance here. You can look at that on your own
charts. I try not to put too many things in front of you to look at, but also leave you somewhere to go in and
do your own investigation. So we have a weekly Fair Value Gap here again. Look at your weekly chart, and
you'll see this same thing here.
Easiest way to identify it with study is: put a rectangle on the daily chart from this low to this candle's high,
and then go to your weekly chart, and you'll see right away what that weekly Fair Value Gap is. So the Fair
Value Gap is formed now because of this weak close.
We've worked off of this Fair Value Gap, and I want to see: does this find its way above the weekly Fair
Value Gap and act as support?

There's been some issues with the crops from an agricultural standpoint for folks that follow the commodity
market, the grain markets, because there's a drought right now. Corn, wheat, and soybeans are expected
to have a difficult year, so that would be bullish for those grain prices. And if that's the case, that would
probably be seen with a Dollar Index that is consolidating or lower.
But we have the added uncertainty of war, if you're paying attention. So there's a lot of things to weigh out.
What do you do with this information? When I see it like this, I stay to the day trading side of the market,
not doing longer-term analysis. Sticking to intraday trading the sessions only: morning session, afternoon
session, London session, that type of thing.
EUR/

On the hourly chart here, you can see we ran above buy side, a shift in market structure right there, and
then we rallied back up. This is Model 2022. Here's your entry, and then drawing down into an imbalance.
That would be your perfect 2022 Model.
Market trades all the way down into this low here. If you look real close, you'll see there is a sell-side
imbalance, buy-side inefficiency (SIBI) with an order block. That's why you see this nice little rip higher.
There is some unfilled sell-side imbalance, buy-side inefficiency. So for the bulls, you would need to get
back above the weekly Fair Value Gap, find some support, and rally. Otherwise, you know, we could see
consolidation on the dollar and make a run for Euro sell-side liquidity here and dig in a little bit deeper on
that weekly Fair Value Gap that has just formed in a time frame.
What is a Breakaway Gap? A Breakaway Gap is when we have a SIBI or BISS (Buy-side Imbalance Sell
- side Inefficiency) in an area close to a swing high or low. In this case, it's moving lower, so in this swing
high, if this is an optimal strategy that you would look to try to get into, obviously you would expect it to do
what? Drop.

section_title: BREAKAWAY GAP CHARACTERISTICS AND TRADING IMPLICATIONS
content:
If it drops aggressively, that's a very good signature, something that supports the idea that you're on side.
You don't want to see that gap filled, so you would treat it as a Breakaway Gap. And it ran down into the
108.45s or so.

E-mini S&P weekly chart. So we had our inefficiency here, and I tell you this old high and buy side liquidity,
I even mentioned all these things should all be repriced to the entirety of this SIBI. And there is a Volume
Imbalance right there that should resume going higher. Coming into the next week or later on, that would
be the next draw.
Comparatively speaking, as I mentioned, also I won't be talking about NASDAQ tonight, but NASDAQ has
in fact reached up into the equivalent of this Volume Imbalance on ES here. The NASDAQ for that same
time period in that respective date, it did already trade up into that area for its Volume Imbalance, but the
S&P has only traded up into this SIBI with buy side being taken here. And we traded right back down into
the low of this SIBI right there.
ES 1
- HOUR CHART:

section_title: OPENING RANGE GAPS AND LIQUIDITY VOIDS
content:
One hour chart on ES, and the chart is shown in regular trading hours. Here's that buy side we've worked
all up to the upside for a move just outside of that old SIBI, which is the shaded area here. Then we broke
lower. Heavy Volume Imbalance in here. It traded up just enough to get into the actual gap. This is a
Liquidity Void, a real one where there's no trading at all.
And then another Opening Range Gap. That's what you're seeing here. These are Opening Range Gaps:
Opening Range Gap, Opening Range Gap. So where we settle at on the New York session, when you're
watching CNBC and they ring the bell and they clap your hands that's the equivalent. And then where we
open up the following day at 9:30, that's what's being represented here that's the Opening Range Gap.

section_title: OPENING RANGE GAP (ORG) CHARACTERISTICS AND EXPIRATION
content:
So whenever you hear me talking about that, that's the concept I'm employing here and in reference to
time. So there's an Opening Gap here, trade up into it, treat it once more as resistance, and it sells off.
Opening Range Gap for Thursday's trading, and then Friday morning 9:30 opening. That separation in
there will be seen as an Opening Range Gap.
All right, so you can see a nice little Opening Range Gap. Trades up into here on the 21st, you break lower.
We have an Opening Range Gap here that's not shaded, but extend that in time, you can see that we're
returning back into that.
Now, Opening Range Gaps: I don't like to take them beyond five days. So, in my opinion, they expire in five
days. Now, that doesn't mean that you won't find examples of where the market has respected them that
were utilized beyond the limitations of the filter I'm saying I use for my analysis. So five days after that, you
know, six days from this day, basically, I'm no longer interested in using that as a return back into it as
support or resistance, so I'm not treating it, you know, like a New Week Opening Gap. So New Week
Opening Gaps, I can hold on to them and use them for months. Opening Range Gaps and New Day
Opening Gaps, they're short-term imbalances with an expiration date, and it's five days, okay?
ES 5
- MINUTE CHART:

section_title: FRIDAY'S PRICE ACTION AND FVG TARGET
content:
So, we had the market open, bounce around, trade up into half of the gap from the Opening Range. And
we have a five minute chart here. As you can see, where we settled, where we opened, that range here,
and then 50% of that Opening Range Gap is being noted here. So we traded above the short-term high in
the morning session, trade back down to the low of the Opening Range Gap, which is the opening price
9:30 New York local time regular trading hours. You'll see the separation. Check down to that opening price
at 9:30, and then it rallies.
What is it going to try to do? It's going to reach for the buy side here and then try to work inside and reprice
into what? All this Opening Range Gap. But we have a lot of order flow here and here, so it's not necessary
to do a complete closure. And I mentioned that I was interested in that daily Fair Value Gap on ES. I sent a
tweet out on Friday morning, and I'll show you that when we get into later, but right away you can see how
we went from noon to 1:30. We ran buy side, and then the target or bias that I mentioned I liked for Friday,
which was the discount Fair Value Gap on ES, we actually traded into it by one tick.

- 1:30 PM) AND INVERSION APPLICATION
So here's the 12 P.M. to 1:30 P.M. New York lunch. I taught recently how to use this information. If you have
a bias, you think it's going to be drawing to a specific higher time frame key level. I shared that with you on
Twitter Friday morning: I favored the lower Fair Value Gap, and you can see that on the ES chart. That's
what we're looking at here.
So we ran up throughout lunch to take the buy side half of right here, half of the Opening Range Gap
between where we settled on the previous day and when we opened at 9:30 on Friday. Measure that with a
fib, 50% is here. It trades to it here, consolidates, and one more time runs into it. And then we change into
the Mean Threshold of this old shorter block. So notice how it was used here as support, rallied up. Once
we traded below that low, every FVG (Fair Value Gap) or displacement array has an Inversion Application.
Everything acts like you learned about in terms of support and resistance.

section_title: ICT VS. CLASSIC SUPPORT/RESISTANCE AND FINITE LEVELS
content:
Which high and low do you use with classic support resistance? See, I've already deciphered all that. I'm
not looking at just the low, seeing classic support and resistance levels. They'll look at that low, draw a line
on that time and price, stop there, but it doesn't. And it consolidates and rips through it. So my ability to go
into Order Flow, old market structure, and refer to very specific levels, things I'm looking for that are very
finite they're not ambiguous, that's not a range that you have to guess about. So a lot of these things
have required rules to adhere to, and you're learning them as we go through this year.
So the market rolls back on anyone that was short in this big gap down. With electronic trading hour, you
would see there's a lot of movement from here to here. Buy side here, buy side above some other highs
you can hear that we don't even need to look at. But to eliminate all the guesswork, just get a range on that
Opening Range Gap. It's Friday, it's likely to draw down into its daily Fair Value Gap, which I'll show you. So
it's only pulled back to give a short-term premium, then the market breaks lower. And then you get the
typical run back down into the Opening Range Gap low here, and then trades into the low target, which is
the daily Fair Value Gap. And again, I'll show you what that is. But you also probably notice that you see
some discoloration here. For those that have been with me for this year, you already know what that is:
that's the framework for the Silver Bullet AM and PM session.

- 11 AM) ON 1-MINUTE CHART
So here's a one minute chart, and you can see how that 50% of the Order Block and convergence of the
Opening Range. The dashed line is 50% of the Opening Range Gap, and this is 50% of the Order Block or
Mean Threshold. So that's what you're seeing here. I'm just annotating that. I would have done a better job
of not working it that way so you're looking at it now. I wish I said it differently, but we're going to stick with
what we got here.
So at 10 o'clock in the morning until 11 o'clock in the morning, we're looking for a Silver Bullet. I mentioned
it, I favored the lower end, so bias in my opinion was bearish. And I know some of you that are highly
critical, or just maybe you're brand new, you're looking at this move here to here: 'Why is it bearish when I
did this run, or how's it bearish when it did this run to here or from here to here?' What was the largest
move of the day? It was moving from here down to here, and it went one tick below where I've already told
you to look at on Twitter with the daily Fair Value Gap. So bias is what? My bias is bearish, so I wanted to
go short.

section_title: SILVER BULLET AM TRADE EXECUTION AND LUNCH RETRACEMENT
content:
I showed examples of executing, working at the 9:30 opening, and was like 75 or so handles taken out. You
can look at that on Twitter, by the way. But if you want to find out what my Twitter account is, you can go to
my YouTube channel and you'll see the actual official Twitter link in the upper right hand corner.
But we have a gap here, we trade up into it right there. And at 11 o'clock, a few minutes after, it still went a
little bit higher, but that's fine. You can see on the one minute chart there's a little bit longer SIBI, which is a
Fair Value Gap, so it offers it there that you would have been filled here. And you'd have to have a little bit
of drawdown.
Is that the rules for Silver Bullet this year? You have to enter between 10 o'clock and 11 o'clock. If you're
very uncomfortable with that idea, then don't trade that model. And it trades down to the low end of the
Opening Range Gap, so that's almost about 10 handles. But we don't need that; we just need five handles
for a Silver Bullet that would have offered it there. Then we have the retracement going into lunch, running
on buy side.

- 3 PM) AND DAILY FVG TARGET
And then we see at 1:30 we get one more little trap move that takes us above the relative equal highs in
here. Buy side is tripped and displacement is shown. Short-term lows, Fair Value Gap trades up into it, wait
for displacement. We're at 50% of the Opening Range, and we're at the old swing low, which is a down
- closed candle, bullish Order Block and Mean Threshold level. Okay, so that's what this is. The market starts
to work lower again. Time between two o'clock and three o'clock, we're looking for an imbalance that we
can use to enter on to get in sync with the directional bias I've shared mine on Twitter.
So here's a SIBI, trade up into it between two o'clock and three o'clock there's your PM session Silver
Bullet. What do you hold for? Open Range Gap low and then the daily Fair Value Gap. So we had a nice
run lower here, and then we dig below these relative equal lows and one tick into that daily Fair Value Gap,
and then retracing back up into the range.
CONCLUSION:

section_title: ES DAILY FAIR VALUE GAP DELIVERY
content:
Here is that Fair Value Gap I mentioned in the daily chart for ES. I had this one highlighted, but I very
specifically stated that I favor the lower as my bias on the day, and it drove down in one tick below that low
right there. That was the delivery. So 4381.75. 4381.50 was the printed level today. There's your difference
right there.
Hope you found this insightful. I'll try to be as direct and to the point as possible. Enjoy your weekend, and
I'll touch base with you next week. I'll talk to you next time. Be safe.
